title: Sub-lemma: single determinant-shell support (collapse to a nonzero monomial)

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
**Claim needed.** For the chosen $W$ and a suitable $V$, the integrand $g\mapsto W(\operatorname{diag}(g,1)u_Q)\,V(g)$ is supported (mod $N_n$) on a **single determinant shell** $S=\{|\det g|=c\}$ and is right-invariant under a compact-open subgroup there. Then by $|\det g|^{s-1/2}$-homogeneity,
$$\Psi(s;W,V)=c_0\cdot c^{\,s-1/2},$$
a monomial in $q^{\pm s}$, hence **entire and nowhere vanishing iff $c_0\ne0$**. This is the cleanest way to get 'finite and nonzero for all $s$': force the Rankin–Selberg $L$-factor to a unit.

**Documented danger (from first_proof_1, FATAL).** A previous attempt claimed single-shell support but the honest computation gave support on the **range** $\{|Q|\le|y|\le|Q|q^{n}\}$ — a *multi-shell* function — because $1/L(s,\pi)$ is a degree-$\le n$ polynomial in $q^{-s}$ with generically nonzero coefficients. Reducing to one shell requires genuinely *killing* the extra shells, i.e. showing $L(s,\Pi\times\pi)$ collapses to a monomial unit. This step is the mathematical heart and must be proved, not asserted.

**To prove.** Either (a) directly compute the unfolded integrand and exhibit single-shell support, or (b) explain the multi-shell cancellation rigorously. The shift $u_Q$ with $|Q|=q^{a(\pi)}$ is the device aligning the support shell with the level of the newvector $V^\circ$ of $\pi$.
